conversation.message: i have been smoking for 10 years and my doctor says i should quit
It sounds like you're taking your doctor's advice seriously, and that's a great first step. What's been going through your mind when your doctor told you it's time to quit?
i felt sacred . my cough has been getting worse and my blood pressure is also high
It sounds like you're feeling a mix of emotions and concerns about your health. Are you worried that giving up cigarettes might make your symptoms, like your cough and high blood pressure, worse?
no i know quitting will make me feel better

You're clear that quitting will make you feel better, and that's a strong motivator. How do you think you'll handle situations where you feel the urge to smoke, like when you're stressed or around people who smoke?
That's what worries me. I smoke a lot when I'm stressed at work and after meals. I don't know what to do in those moments

You're sharing your concerns about how you'll handle situations that trigger your urge to smoke, and that's really helpful. What's been going through your mind when you're feeling stressed at work, and how do you think that might be connected to your desire to smoke?
When I'm stressed I just need something to do with my hands and the break outside helps me reset. It's become my way of coping

You're saying that when you're stressed, you need something to do with your hands and a break outside helps you reset. It's become your way of coping. How might you be able to substitute the break outside with something else that still helps you reset and keep your hands busy?
I'm not sure, maybe chewing gum or going for a walk? But I haven't really thought about quitting seriously yet, I just came because my doctor told me to

You're considering some alternatives to smoking, like chewing gum or going for a walk, which is a great start. How do you think your doctor's advice factored into your decision to come in and talk about quitting, given that you haven't been thinking about quitting on your own?
I guess the doctor scared me enough to take it seriously. I'll try the gum idea when I feel the urge

You're feeling a bit more motivated to try some strategies to quit smoking after your doctor's advice, and you're willing to try the gum idea when you feel the urge. What do you think would be the best time for you to start trying the gum, so it becomes a habit and helps you feel more prepared for your next craving?
my friends smoke and i kind of picked it up 3 years ago. i dont think im addicted or anything. i can stop whenever i want.
It sounds like you started smoking around your friends and aren't sure if you're addicted. Have you ever tried quitting or cutting down on smoking before, even for just a day?
No never tried. I smoke maybe 4-5 a day, only when I'm with friends or when I'm bored and stressed

It sounds like you smoke relatively lightly and mainly in social situations or when you're feeling bored or stressed. Can you tell me a little bit more about what it is about being with your friends that makes you want to smoke?
I don't know, everyone just smokes and I join in. It feels weird to say no. Anyway what can I do to stop craving it when I'm bored?

It sounds like you feel pressure to conform with your friends when they're smoking, and that's making it hard for you to say no. Are there any other situations where you feel like you'd want to smoke when you're bored, besides hanging out with friends?
no
It sounds like you're having trouble responding, do you want to talk about what's on your mind and how it relates to your smoking?
Sorry I meant I don't know how to say no to friends. What can I do when I feel bored and want to smoke
It sounds like saying no to your friends when they're smoking is a big challenge for you. Can you think of a simple phrase that might help you politely say no when they offer you a cigarette?
no i cannot do that

The patient is expressing frustration and feeling unable to say no to friends when they're smoking. 

What's one thing you think would happen if you did say no to your friends when they offered you a cigarette?
They'd probably think I'm being weird. I don't really want to quit anyway, I'm fine
It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about quitting and worried about what others might think. Can you tell me more about what's making you say "I don't really want to quit anyway"?
